words: SAM SCHRAGER: The following report made by Carol Ryrie Brink at her home in
Pacific Beach, California in June 1975. This was done in response to the
questions recorded at the beginning of the tape by Sam Schrager.

speaker: SS
words: I think it might be interesting for you to discuss, but please don't feel

speaker: SS
words: bound to these in any way, if they don't strike your fancy. And please feel

speaker: SS
words: free to discuss anything that you want to.

speaker: SS
words: In your letter you say that it might be good to get down for posterity

speaker: SS
words: that your books are not real history and that they're just fictionalized to

speaker: SS
words: a great extent. I think it would be interesting for you to discuss this in

speaker: SS
words: general, and also, with regard to some specific plots and characters. What

speaker: SS
words: are some of the sources that you've drawn from and events and people in the

speaker: SS
words: history of this area, and how did you choose to transform and use them in

speaker: SS
words: your fiction. One particular instance that comes to my mind is the case of

speaker: SS
words: Winnie Booth and Dr. Ledbrooke, who are clearly models for Jennie Waldon

speaker: SS
words: and Hugh Allerton in Buffalo Coat. This remains a most puzzling occurrence

speaker: SS
words: in Moscow history, and I am curious about what you heard about the double

speaker: SS
words: suicide and what you regard as the factual content of your account. Your

speaker: SS
words: explanation of how it happened is very plausible.

speaker: SS
words: Of course, your grandmother, Mrs. Watkins, figures very prominently

speaker: SS
words: in a number of your books, and I think it would be very interesting if you

speaker: SS
words: discuss her importance to you and the kind of person she was. In Buffalo

speaker: SS
words: Coat and Snow in the River and Caddie Woodlawn, too, she is an exceptionally

speaker: SS
words: strong, wise and kind person. One of the most provocative passages in Caddy

speaker: SS
words: Woodlawn, for me, is when Caddy's father comforts her that night when she

speaker: SS
words: is planning to run away, telling her that as a woman her task will be to teach

speaker: SS
words: gentleness and courtesy and love and kindness. I'd like to ask you about

speaker: SS
words: your view of women's roles in pioneer society, and, of course, I know that

speaker: SS
words: this can only be in general terms; but were women and men on anything like

speaker: SS
words: an equal footing? Was Caddy's free life as a tomD°y unusual exception to

speaker: SS
words: the rule? Do you think that many women had difficulty finding fulfilment

speaker: SS
words: as wives and mothers? Or did they find — did many find this restrictive?

speaker: SS
words: Also, do you think women do play the civilizing role that Mr. Woodlawn describes to

speaker: SS
words: Caddy? Do you think there was a great deal of difference between

speaker: SS
words: the East and the pioneer West in the roles of women? And do you know how

speaker: SS
words: people looked at the question of women*s rights co vote in this area?

speaker: SS
words: Now I know that's quite a few questions, but here's another aspect of—

speaker: SS
words: that I think is interesting— and that's the question of status in pioneer

speaker: SS
words: Moscow. It's a hard thing, I think to get at. I'm wondering if there was

speaker: SS
words: much of a dividing line between, let's say the Ladies Aid and the ordinary

speaker: SS
words: housewives— between people who were rather well off and those who were just

speaker: SS
words: struggling to get by? I'm wondering— do you think status depended more on

speaker: SS
words: a person's accomplishments in this country or more on his background before

speaker: SS
words: coming here? Now as I read your books I see a real conflict in many places

speaker: SS
words: between the opportunities and freedom in this country on one hand, and it's

speaker: SS
words: limitations and the reversals people experienced here on the other. This

speaker: SS
words: seemed especially strong to me in Snow on the River, and I'm wondering if

speaker: SS
words: you'd be interested in discussing that.

speaker: SS
words: Now one historical question is interesting and one that we have a hard

speaker: SS
words: time finding out about, is that of homesteading east of Clarkia towards the

speaker: SS
words: Clearwater. I've read that your aunt homesteaded there. I think it's especially interesting

speaker: SS
words: that a significant number of women took homesteads there.

speaker: SS
words: Now the conflict that you present in Strangers in the Forest which is the

speaker: SS
words: ethics of homesteading with the purpose of selling to the timber companies;

speaker: SS
words: Was that a real issue of the time in this area, do you think? And, I also

speaker: SS
words: think it would be most interesting if you would discuss how you became interested

speaker: SS
words: in writing, and how it relates to your personal background. Do you

speaker: SS
words: have strong feelings about why this area has given you inspiration and has

speaker: SS
words: been the setting for a number of your books?

speaker: SS
words: And one more question: Do you feel that a woman you ve been helped or

speaker: SS
words: hindered by society in your writing career?

speaker: SS
words: Okay, those are just some things that I am throwing out to you and I

speaker: SS
words: hope you really enjoy sitting down and making a tape, and I very much look

speaker: SS
words: forward to hearing it when you send it back to us. So thanks, very much.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BRINK: Good morning, Mr. Schrager, you've asked me a lot of very good questions

speaker: SS
words: and you may find that you've got a monster by the tail when I get started talking,

speaker: SS
words: why there's no way of stopping me, especially about Moscow in the

speaker: SS
words: old days.

speaker: SS
words: I think first, I might tell you a little bit about how I started writing and why I

speaker: SS
words: like to use Idaho as a source of material.

speaker: SS
words: I lost both of my parents by the time I was eight years old, and I went

speaker: SS
words: to live with this very wonderful grandmother and aunt. We were three lone

speaker: SS
words: women in a big house. I often was very lonely as a child, although I had a

speaker: SS
words: happy childhood, really. But I had to make my own amusements. I had a pony

speaker: SS
words: and I rode all over the Idaho countryside on my pony, usually making up stories for

speaker: SS
words: myself as I went along. Incidentally both my grandmother and aunt

speaker: SS
words: were very good storytellers; they liked to recount the stories of the things

speaker: SS
words: that had happened in the small town, and I listened and took it all in, and

speaker: SS
words: then I liked to make up my own stories. I had to amuse myself in various

speaker: SS
words: ways. I read a great deal; liked books. I decided I wanted to be a writer

speaker: SS
words: and at that time, I thought I'4 like to be an illustrator too. But that

speaker: SS
words: ambition fell by the wayside, although I do still like to draw and paint.

speaker: SS
words: But I think sometimes that if I had been born into a big, lively, jolly family, I

speaker: SS
words: might never have become a writer. It's one of those things that—

speaker: SS
words: well, lonely children make their own amusements and often it's very valuable

speaker: SS
words: to them in later life. Also if you have a feeling of being different from

speaker: SS
words: the other children that you know, it's likely to make either a criminal or

speaker: SS
words: a genius out of you, and I've fallen sort of between the two benches, I

speaker: SS
words: don't think I qualify in either respect. But has influenced my writing

speaker: SS
words: very much that I had a very observant but lonely childhood in a small town

speaker: SS
words: where everybody knew about everybody else. So, I think although I've used

speaker: SS
words: other backgrounds in my books, the dominant source of material has always

speaker: SS
words: been my experiences as a child in Idaho. I'm working on a book now that may

speaker: SS
words: never see the light of day, but it's important to me. I'm calling it Unimportant People

speaker: SS
words: and they're people that I knew in a small town where I grew

speaker: SS
words: up and some of the true stories rather than the fictionalized ones, because

speaker: SS
words: I have fictionalized many of the familiar stories of Moscow in my books.

speaker: SS
words: I don't have total recall, by any means, but I do have a very good memory of my grandmother,

speaker: SS
words: especially of my early childhood. As I say, I've used

speaker: SS
words: that very much in my books. It might be well here to point out some of the

speaker: SS
words: differences between the real facts and the fiction in my books. For instance,

speaker: SS
words: the story of my grandfather and his assassination— that's part of Moscow

speaker: SS
words: history now— but many people feel that Buffalo Coat exactly describes that.

speaker: SS
words: That's not true. Much of it is fact, but it's fictionalized to a great extent.

speaker: SS
words: I have notes, for instances, on the— my grandfather's office. This was a

speaker: SS
words: small building just off Main Street, and it was occupied by a number of different

speaker: SS
words: doctors. I might just give you a bit of that history as it really

speaker: SS
words: occurred: My grandfather built the building, I believe. It was a very small

speaker: SS
words: building— brick building— and I'm not sure that it still exists. For a

speaker: SS
words: long time he was assisted by a French doctor, named Doctor Dillipine and

speaker: SS
words: Doctor Dillipine was one of the first specialists in Moscow. He specialized

speaker: SS
words: in diseases of the eye, ear, nose and throat. He had been married to an English woman

speaker: SS
words: and had practiced in England. It's very interesting to me and

speaker: SS
words: has been hard for me to understand how so many people from far away came to

speaker: SS
words: Idaho in those days, but it was the days when everybody was going West and

speaker: SS
words: we had many Scandinavians and people from England, people from France, people

speaker: SS
words: from all parts of the world. I suppose people from Russia, at least I sup

speaker: SS
words: pose that's how the town got it's name, although I've never known any of them.

speaker: SS
words: Well, Doctor Dillipine as I said, was French, but he'd practiced in England

speaker: SS
words: and had been married to an English woman who died, I believe, when his

speaker: SS
words: son was born. He had twin girls and a little boy. And for a while he brought

speaker: SS
words: them to Moscow and they lived with my grandparents and were almost like their

speaker: SS
words: own children. They all thought a great deal of Doctor Dillipine. But before

speaker: SS
words: Doctor Watkins, my grandfather, was killed, Doctor Dillipine left Moscow and

speaker: SS
words: returned to England with his family. And then, there was, of course, the

speaker: SS
words: killing of Doctor Watkins and there were other men in town who were either

speaker: SS
words: wounded or lost their lives at the same time, and that, as I have described

speaker: SS
words: it in Buffalo Coat, I believe, is very nearly correct. But I am going on

speaker: SS
words: with the notes on Doctor Watkins' office now just to keep the historical

speaker: SS
words: order proper.1 So after my grandfather's death, his office was rented by another

speaker: SS
words: doctor who had just come from England, and this was Doctor Francis J.

speaker: SS
words: Ledbrooke, who came and brought his English wife with him.

speaker: SS
words: As you know, of course, in history on May 12, 1902, Doctor Ledbrooke and Winnifred

speaker: SS
words: Booth,

speaker: SS
words: the daughter of the Methodist minister died together in a suicide pact at

speaker: SS
words: Orofino, Idaho. Now there have been very many accounts of this double tragedy,

speaker: SS
words: and the newspapers of the time were full of the fact that Doctor Ledbrooke had a malign

speaker: SS
words: hypnotic influence over his victim and that he was really

speaker: SS
words: a villain who murdered this beautiful girl and people didn't quite understand

speaker: SS
words: it all. This all happened after my grandfather's death. Now in Buffalo

speaker: SS
words: Coat I make these two men contemporaries and rivals, so that you see the book

speaker: SS
words: is fictionalized very much to that extent. Mrs. Ledbrooke, the wife of the

speaker: SS
words: doctor lived for many years with my grandmother. My grandmother was a wonderful person

speaker: SS
words: for taking in people who needed help and making them part of

speaker: SS
words: her family. And I loved Leddy, as I called her, very much as a child. She

speaker: SS
words: was one of the plainest, ugliest— is a bad word— because she had a good

speaker: SS
words: disposition, but she was not a beautiful woman in any way, and I think that

speaker: SS
words: my interpretation of the double suicide is perhaps about as near as you can

speaker: SS
words: come to the truth of the matter. I think that Doctor Ledbrooke and Winnie

speaker: SS
words: Booth were both very much infatuated with each other. At that time, living

speaker: SS
words: in sin was one of the things that religious people did not do, so what did

speaker: SS
words: they do but decide that if they couldn't live together, they couldn't live

speaker: SS
words: at all. I think that's the real explanation of it. And, my grandmother,

speaker: SS
words: I'm sure felt that way. Nobody in our family felt that Doctor Ledbrooke

speaker: SS
words: had any malign, hypnotic influence over his victim as the newspapers would

speaker: SS
words: like to have had you believe. I think it was purely an unfortunate love

speaker: SS
words: affair, a sort of Romeo and Juliet effect, but Romeo was married, and that

speaker: SS
words: made things very difficult in those days. Now-a-days I am sure people would

speaker: SS
words: have had a divorce, would have taken it all much more lightly and not felt

speaker: SS
words: that death was the only solution. As I say, I loved Mrs. Ledbrooke very

speaker: SS
words: much. And she was an eccentric character but a very dear person, and, in

speaker: SS
words: fact, she used to read aloud to me— when anything that anybody would read

speaker: SS
words: aloud to me was so much fun that I would sit at her feet on . stool and

speaker: SS
words: she had a friend who owned a complete set of Elsie Dinsmore books. They

speaker: SS
words: are horrible books and I knew it at the time, but the fact that she would

speaker: SS
words: read them aloud to me was so delightful that I would sit by the hour and

speaker: SS
words: eat her peppermints that she kept in a tin box, and listen to Elsie Dinsmore

speaker: SS
words: just because it was so delightful to have somebody read aloud to me.

speaker: SS
words: We always had dogs and she used to rattle her tin box of candies and the

speaker: SS
words: dog would rush to her room barking and wanting a treat and then she would

speaker: SS
words: take ^for a walk. Altogether, she was quite a delightful character, and

speaker: SS
words: I have not really made the best of her in Buffalo Coat. But all of this

speaker: SS
words: happened after my grandfather's death, rather than at the same time that

speaker: SS
words: he was living.

speaker: SS
words: Then after Doctor Ledbrooke's tragedy, Doctor C. D. Parsons moved into

speaker: SS
words: the office. And on a day in May in 1903 he fell from a packhorse while he

speaker: SS
words: was on a camping trip and he died from a fractured skull. Well, by that

speaker: SS
words: time, people in Moscow were sure that there was a hoodoo on Doctor Watkins'

speaker: SS
words: office and no one would rent it. My poor grandmother really needed the rent

speaker: SS
words: because just before her husband's death he had cancelled all of his insurance policies

speaker: SS
words: because he felt that he was so husky and strong that there

speaker: SS
words: was no use paying out these foolish premiums, so that she had to live pretty much

speaker: SS
words: on the rent$ of a few small pieces of property that she owned. And

speaker: SS
words: the doctor's office stood empty for a long time because it seemed to be

speaker: SS
words: hoodooed. Nobody wanted to rent it. But then Doctor "tMl.f.r* came back

speaker: SS
words: again to Moscow; he was the French doctor who had assisted my grandfather

speaker: SS
words: years before. And he had no superstitions apparently about the office, he

speaker: SS
words: rented it again and apparently did very well for a great many years. And

speaker: SS
words: many people, I am sure, of the older generation may remember him as a successful and

speaker: SS
words: good doctor. I have somewhat maligned him in Buffalo Coat too,

speaker: SS
words: because while I am sure that there was a slight romantic interest between

speaker: SS
words: him and my grandmother, he was not a complete drunkard as I have suggested

speaker: SS
words: in the book. So you see that one takes what's at hand and makes it into

speaker: SS
words: a story. I might say that I first wrote a novel based on the doctor's

speaker: SS
words: office. It followed somewhat the lines of this little resume that I have

speaker: SS
words: just given you, and I sent it into my publisher, it was the first novel I'd

speaker: SS
words: done, I'd done children's books before that, and they sent it back saying,

speaker: SS
words: "You have good material here, but this is not a novel, it's three short

speaker: SS
words: stories, and you kill off the most interesting character at the end of the

speaker: SS
words: first short story." Meaning my grandfather. So, after that I in order

speaker: SS
words: to make a novel of it— I had to make these men contemporaries and had to

speaker: SS
words: weave the whole plot together as it appears in the book, Buffalo Coat.

speaker: SS
words: And that's why that people should not take my books as genuine history.

speaker: SS
words: They're based on history, and some of it is very real, but in order to make

speaker: SS
words: a good story of it a novelist starts with what's real and goes on and

speaker: SS
words: uses his imagination to build up a story that will interest people who are

speaker: SS
words: wanting to read a good story.

speaker: SS
words: While I am on the subject of my grandfather I might give you a few

speaker: SS
words: notes, factual notes, on his life. You may have these in other places, I

speaker: SS
words: am not sure how the records go, but William Woodbury Watkins was born in Warner, New

speaker: SS
words: Hampshire on August

speaker: SS
words: 3, 1846. His father was of Welsh background and his mother was a cousin of her name

speaker: SS
words: was Phoebe Abbott— she was a cousin of Jacob Abbott and John S. C.

speaker: SS
words: Abbott, who were both best-selling authors of their day. And Lyman Abbott,

speaker: SS
words: the celebrated New England preacher was ^ second cousin and Emma Abbott, the

speaker: SS
words: singer was a close connection. So he had a good background on his mother's

speaker: SS
words: side, I'm not so sure about his father, who was, apparently, a rather cantankerous

speaker: SS
words: old man. My grandfather, William Watkins, was the youngest of a

speaker: SS
words: large family of children, and his mother, probably worn out by the time he

speaker: SS
words: arrived, died when he was quite small, and he was reared by older sisters.

speaker: SS
words: His father was either unable or unwilling to give him financial assistance

speaker: SS
words: with his education and he never went to high school as my aunt used to tell

speaker: SS
words: me^ But somehow, he went South and there a man named Duke became very much

speaker: SS
words: interested in him and financed his medical education. He was accepted at

speaker: SS
words: the St. Louis Medical University without high school credits, and he apparently graduated

speaker: SS
words: there in good standing, and later served for a while on

speaker: SS
words: the faculty as a lecturer on women's and children's diseases. He was dark,

speaker: SS
words: had flashing black eyes and usually mutton-chop sideburns or walrus moustache.

speaker: SS
words: He was a very vigorous man. People who are interested in horoscopes would

speaker: SS
words: say that he was a perfect Leo, he was born in August, early August, and he

speaker: SS
words: was a very dominant, vigorous character. People reacted quite strongly to

speaker: SS
words: ward him; either they adored him or they disliked him very much. The final

speaker: SS
words: tragedy was caused by the fact that he had been asked to reprimand a young

speaker: SS
words: man, named William Stephans for beating his mother, they both belonged to

speaker: SS
words: the MasonSLodge and the Masons had asked my grandfather to go out and tell

speaker: SS
words: this young man that it wasn't nice to beat his mother, and apparently he

speaker: SS
words: did so. And that was one reason why he was put on the list of people that

speaker: SS
words: Stephans wanted to shoot ^the day he came in town, and he was the first one

speaker: SS
words: that Stephans met on his way in town and he was shot and instantly killed.

speaker: SS
words: He married my grandmother, Caroline Augusta Woodhouse in St. Louis when

speaker: SS
words: fcney were young people. My grandmother used to tell me that she was shelling peas

speaker: SS
words: on the side porch at their house when he proposed to her. And

speaker: SS
words: it was rather ironical that when they came to tell her of his death, she

speaker: SS
words: was sitting on the side porch of her house in Idaho shelling peas into a

speaker: SS
words: pan. They had eight children; two boys and six girls, but only three survived to adulthood.

speaker: SS
words: This also is ironical, because my grandfather was a

speaker: SS
words: doctor especially of women's and children's diseases. But in those days

speaker: SS
words: nobody, even the best of doctors, knew how to cope with diphtheria and

speaker: SS
words: scarlet fever and some of the scourges of childhood. So that while he

speaker: SS
words: delivered many babies and was a perfectly successful doctor, as far as his

speaker: SS
words: knowledge went in those days, he was still unable to save his two little

speaker: SS
words: boys and three little girls. He did, however— I have a great sense of

speaker: SS
words: gratitude for him because he apparently saved my life. My grandmother

speaker: SS
words: said that they thought at first I was stillborn, but my grandfather

speaker: SS
words: took me up and worked my arms up and down and blew his own breath, doubt

speaker: SS
words: less tobacco scented because he was a great cigar smoker, into my lungs

speaker: SS
words: and started me going on a life that I've enjoyed very much, indeed. So

speaker: SS
words: I feel that I owe him as much as I owe my mother and father for my being

speaker: SS
words: and having a very happy and successful life.

speaker: SS
words: Dr. Watkins kept a record book of the six hundred and seventy-one babies

speaker: SS
words: he delivered during his career. And he practiced first in various places

speaker: SS
words: in Missouri and in Kansas. And then in August, again, which is his month

speaker: SS
words: the month he was born in, the month he was killed in, and the month he moved to Idaho

speaker: SS
words: in 1887, when they came to Idaho. And the Indians at that

speaker: SS
words: time used to come and camp on the hill just up near the Presbyterian Church

speaker: SS
words: and where they built their little house, which they later enlarged. My

speaker: SS
words: grandfather, as it tells in Buffalo Coat was instrumental in getting the

speaker: SS
words: bell for the Presbyterian Church, and he wanted the largest one in town.

speaker: SS
words: Moscow used to be a great town for church bells. I don't know that they

speaker: SS
words: still ring so strenuously orv Sunday morning, but churches were proud of

speaker: SS
words: their bells, and the morning that he got the biggest bell installed was

speaker: SS
words: to ring for church in the morning, he had a sick headache and his house

speaker: SS
words: was right across the street from the Presbyterian Church, and this bell

speaker: SS
words: began to boom and he jumped out of bed, rushed out and said, "For goodness

speaker: SS
words: sake, what has happened?" And it was vibrating the bedsprings and he was

speaker: SS
words: just in misery with this bell. So he never was very happy with his bell

speaker: SS
words: that he had worked so hard for. He was very forthright and an interesting character.

speaker: SS
words: He was instrumental in helping to get the University of

speaker: SS
words: Idaho for Moscow, although I am sure many people at the time thought that

speaker: SS
words: it belonged in the south where it was available to more Idaho people, and

speaker: SS
words: he was one of the early regents of the University, I think from 1893 to

speaker: SS
words: 1895. And he also gave the first award ever offered at the University of

speaker: SS
words: Idaho, which was the Watkins medal for oratory. And, let's see, what else

speaker: SS
words: about him? I think he was the first president of the Idaho State Medical Association

speaker: SS
words: in 1893-1894. Altogether, he had a great deal to do with

speaker: SS
words: the building of the town and he was a very forthright character. Many people, as I

speaker: SS
words: say, probably disliked him and yet, I remember my grandmother

speaker: SS
words: saying that at his funeral a little girl walked miles from a farm out in

speaker: SS
words: the country to lay a little bouquet of wilted wild flowers on his grave,

speaker: SS
words: and there were many people who were greatly devoted to him. Perhaps

speaker: SS
words: that's enough for my grandfather.

speaker: SS
words: My grandmother was a very — well it's difficult for me to say, be

speaker: SS
words: cause she was the greatest influence in my life— she was full of kindness

speaker: SS
words: and wisdom. I have always felt that^there have been two great influences

speaker: SS
words: in my life; my grandmother and my husband. My relationship to my grand

speaker: SS
words: mother was a very special one. She represented for me stability, security,

speaker: SS
words: wisdom and good sense. These things had been lacking in much of my earliest

speaker: SS
words: life, so I prized them when I found them. In a sense, she also represented

speaker: SS
words: romance for her vivid stories of her own childhood on a pioneer frontier

speaker: SS
words: gave me my first feeling of continuity with the past. A past more ex^citing

speaker: SS
words: and strange than my own. I expect that I have glamorized her more than I

speaker: SS
words: should because she was a quiet old woman who had allowed herself to get too

speaker: SS
words: fat. She seldom left her house to go anywhere, certainly not socially.

speaker: SS
words: Her interests were not broad and her opinions not unusual. A heart condition kept

speaker: SS
words: her at home, but I am sure that a more restless person would

speaker: SS
words: have gone out in spite of an uncertain heart. She had great calm and self-content,

speaker: SS
words: and the world outside had in very many ways treated her so cruelly

speaker: SS
words: that she did well to shun it. She made her own world at home, and if any

speaker: SS
words: one wished to find her there, she was good tempered and available. People

speaker: SS
words: had to come to her. She enjoyed men's chores and was never happier than

speaker: SS
words: when mending a fence, knocking together a chicken coop or repairing a clock

speaker: SS
words: or a door lock, or sharpening a knife, or spading up a garden plot. She

speaker: SS
words: was competent indoors, too, baking bread and large, thick cookies sprinkled

speaker: SS
words: with caraway seeds. She never liked to do fancy work as her sisters used

speaker: SS
words: to do, but she was good at all sorts of utilitarian handiwork. She kept

speaker: SS
words: almost a small farm on the place on Van Buren Street. There were all sorts

speaker: SS
words: of fruit trees and orchards — I understand that Moscow doesn't grow many

speaker: SS
words: fruit trees any more, but in my youth we had wonderful fruity In our back

speaker: SS
words: yard we had two pie cherry trees, a Royal Anne cherry tree, several

speaker: SS
words: other cherry trees, summer apple tree, two prune trees, a pear tree — well

speaker: SS
words: I could go on— it was almost like a little farm. We had a barn, where

speaker: SS
words: they had once kept cows and horses, now I had just a pony there when I lived

speaker: SS
words: at the place. There were chicken runs and we always had chicken for Sunday

speaker: SS
words: dinner if other things failed. And, altogether, it was a very pleasant place

speaker: SS
words: for a child to grow up.

speaker: SS
words: I had lots of friends around the neighborhood and

speaker: SS
words: many of those I have put into my books for children. Three of my books for

speaker: SS
words: children, All Over Town, Two are Better Than One, and Luley , the latest

speaker: SS
words: one, all have Idaho settings. All Over Town is not my own story, its' fictionalized

speaker: SS
words: but it brings in many of the local characters that people remember, such as, Mr. Zumhoff,

speaker: SS
words: the blacksmith who used to shoe my pony and who

speaker: SS
words: was such a hero to all of the children, he was head of the volunteer fire

speaker: SS
words: department and later I knew and was very fond of one of his daughters, but

speaker: SS
words: he was a real town character and there are many others. In Two Are Better

speaker: SS
words: Than One, and Luley I put some of my good friends of my childhood—

speaker: SS
words: (You heard the telephone ring and I had to go and answer it— so I'll try

speaker: SS
words: and take up again.)Two are Better than One and Luley have much to do with

speaker: SS
words: my friends, Charlotte Lewis and Beth Soulen David— Charlotte Lewis Case,

speaker: SS
words: I should say. The M. E. Lewis family was a very important one in the history of Idaho

speaker: SS
words: and I sometimes think that people in Moscow don't know enough

speaker: SS
words: about them or how influential they were socially at the University in many

speaker: SS
words: ways. I think that Mr. Lewis was a banker in Moscow— well, anyway, Char

speaker: SS
words: lotte was a great friend of mine and some of our escapades have gone into

speaker: SS
words: the book called,Two Are Better Than One. Beth Soulen David, who lived next

speaker: SS
words: door to my grandmother's house on Van Buren Street is the heroine of Luley.

speaker: SS
words: I sent her a copy of the book when it came out and said she was to read it

speaker: SS
words: as fiction, but if anything rang a bell with her, why, I hoped she would

speaker: SS
words: enjoy it. And she called me up from Cape Cod where she lives now, and she

speaker: SS
words: and Don were both on the phone, and she said she laughed and cried and real

speaker: SS
words: ly got a good lot of memories out of it. In those books for children I

speaker: SS
words: call Moscow, Warsaw, Idaho. I don't quite know why authors always like to

speaker: SS
words: disguise names a little bit, but we do. In the adult books, I call it Opportunity,

speaker: SS
words: and there's a bit of irony in that because I felt that many of

speaker: SS
words: the people who came there expecting that it was going to be the great opportunity

speaker: SS
words: of the West, felt that in the end they hadn't found what they wanted.

speaker: SS
words: But the fact that Moscow has partly, the fact that it has been so interesting to me

speaker: SS
words: is that there

speaker: SS
words: were very diversified people there. People with all kinds of backgrounds

speaker: SS
words: who came from different parts of the East in the days when to go West was

speaker: SS
words: the thing to do, and I think that made it a very interesting town. Also,

speaker: SS
words: a kind of nervous tension all through the West, these were the adventurous

speaker: SS
words: people who had come West, they weren't the calm stay-at-homes. I think my

speaker: SS
words: grandmother, although she was born in Boston, and spent her childhood in

speaker: SS
words: pioneer Wisconsin, then St. Louis and then Idaho and finally she spent some

speaker: SS
words: time on the far Pacific Coast, so she typifies the pioneer who moved from

speaker: SS
words: the East to the West. But on the other hand, she was a rather quiet person

speaker: SS
words: herself, and I think wouldn't have come if it hadn't been for her pioneering father

speaker: SS
words: and her pioneering husband. But that makes for an interesting

speaker: SS
words: community, I think; people with diverse backgrounds and restless feet who

speaker: SS
words: come together in a little town and perhaps that's why there's so many interesting

speaker: SS
words: stories coming out of Idaho.

speaker: SS
words: You asked about social status in pioneer Moscow. I think there was a

speaker: SS
words: very strong feeling of class. It's rather surprising that there should be

speaker: SS
words: in a Western town, but I feel that there were decide^layers of social acceptability,

speaker: SS
words: that one belonged to the better families and the social life

speaker: SS
words: of the town or one was— belonged to the middle class or the pool hall and

speaker: SS
words: saloon group or the honest Scandinavian farmer group. There were lots of

speaker: SS
words: different levels of social life in the town, and I think those barriers were

speaker: SS
words: harder to cross than they would be today. Many of the Scandinavian farm

speaker: SS
words: girls, who came in to work in the houses of the richer people in town, in a way it

speaker: SS
words: was their education; their going out into the world and learning

speaker: SS
words: how to do things. And now they're the— they became the mothers of some

speaker: SS
words: of the most important and influential businessmen of town, good Christian

speaker: SS
words: leaders. Today, I think there is not the sense of distinction that there

speaker: SS
words: used to be.

speaker: SS
words: You asked also about women's role in pioneer society. I think that women in the West

speaker: SS
words: were much more nearly equal with men than those in the East,

speaker: SS
words: because they had to get out and do hard labor along with the men and they

speaker: SS
words: shared a partnership more truly, I think, than the Eastern women did. We

speaker: SS
words: tend to think of all women being subservient and sort of put-upon in the

speaker: SS
words: Victorian Period. But this isn't true in the West, I am sure. I think the

speaker: SS
words: women worked along with the men and I think the men respected them as competent in

speaker: SS
words: their role. It's true that the women were homemakers and the

speaker: SS
words: men were the adventurers and the builders, but I don't think that there was

speaker: SS
words: nearly the consciousness of difference in the sexes that there is— well,

speaker: SS
words: today we're very conscious of the fact that women should be liberated. In

speaker: SS
words: those days I don't think women cared too much about the vote. Some of them

speaker: SS
words: were very strongly in favor of it, but— and many of them worked hard for

speaker: SS
words: it. But, I don't think that women felt the need of it so much; they worked

speaker: SS
words: along with their men and they usually shared the men's political beliefs.

speaker: SS
words: My great grandmother— my grandmother's mother, was quite a free-thinker,

speaker: SS
words: as they called it in those days. She was a devoted adherent to the doctrines

speaker: SS
words: of Ingersoll, many people thought of him as an atheist and a rather

speaker: SS
words: disreputable influence, but he was quite an intellectual man, too, and she

speaker: SS
words: read his books and^her own thoughts about them. And I think that many

speaker: SS
words: pioneer women did think for themselves and they had no feeling of inferiority. They

speaker: SS
words: felt that they had their role, which was quite as important as

speaker: SS
words: men's role. And you spoke about the scene in which Caddy's father— in

speaker: SS
words: Caddy Woodlawn tells her about the importance of growing up to be a good

speaker: SS
words: woman. I felt that that was quite a real and important scene. My grandmother

speaker: SS
words: was very devoted to her father. She felt about him the way I felt about her.

speaker: SS
words: That he was absolutely just and stable and that whatever his decisions

speaker: SS
words: were, they were tempered by justice, and she could rely on them. I felt

speaker: SS
words: that way about hers always. But the women's libbers a few years ago, put

speaker: SS
words: Caddy Woodlawn on a list of books that they disapproved of. It was in good

speaker: SS
words: company, they had Mary Poppins and Dr. Doolittle and a number of other children's

speaker: SS
words: books on this list. I thought at first that perhaps it was because

speaker: SS
words: they felt that there was bias against the Indians in the book, but, no, it

speaker: SS
words: turned out that they disapproved of Caddy's father urging her to become a

speaker: SS
words: lady. They wanted her to continue being a tomboy all the rest of her life!

speaker: SS
words: I was rather amused by it, but I didn't take it too seriously. But then

speaker: SS
words: fairly recently a friend sent me a clipping from the magazine JW which is

speaker: SS
words: a pro-feminist magazine, and someone had written quite an article in that

speaker: SS
words: in which she'd told about how she had loved Caddy Woodlawn as a child and

speaker: SS
words: that there was no reason why that shouldn't be a book that was very well accepted

speaker: SS
words: by the women's libbers, because it was about a girl who asserted her

speaker: SS
words: own individuality and lived freely with the boys.

speaker: SS
words: And that is true, that my

speaker: SS
words: grandmother grew up with her two brothers doing everything that they did.

speaker: SS
words: She had lost a little sister, Mary, who died after they came out to Wisconsin, and

speaker: SS
words: her father had said that he wanted her to grow up with the boys,

speaker: SS
words: getting her health and strength and that she shouldn't be kept in the house

speaker: SS
words: to do women's tasks. So she really had a real tomboy life. It's interesting now that

speaker: SS
words: while I didn't realize I was writing history when I wrote the

speaker: SS
words: book Caddy Woodlawn, it was only that my grandmother had a very clear memory and she

speaker: SS
words: had told me these stories so many times, that I felt that they

speaker: SS
words: were a part of me, and I wrote down what she had told me. I did consider

speaker: SS
words: able research, too, and we went down into Wisconsin, my husband and I, and

speaker: SS
words: found the old farmhouse where she had lived. We were able to locate it with

speaker: SS
words: tax records at the county seat. The little town of Dunnville, where she

speaker: SS
words: lived when she was a child, and which seemed to promise to become a good

speaker: SS
words: town, was really— now it's a ghost town there, you find the foundations of

speaker: SS
words: the old stores and the taverns and various things. The schoolhouse has

speaker: SS
words: been turned into a private residence. But fortunately the old farmhouses

speaker: SS
words: have escaped the devastation of the years and they are, many of them, still

speaker: SS
words: standing. The house that my grandmother lived in was used to store lumber

speaker: SS
words: and it was just about falling down the Dunn County Historical Society

speaker: SS
words: suddenly became interested in saving this old place, and they purchased a

speaker: SS
words: corner of what was once my great grandfather's farm and moved the house a

speaker: SS
words: few hundred feet onto this part of the property that they had purchased and

speaker: SS
words: they'^restored it and they've made a park of it now with a big state park

speaker: SS
words: marker. And it's beautifully kept up now. They haven't been able to furnish the house

speaker: SS
words: because they don't have a permanent caretaker there, but

speaker: SS
words: the house is still available for people to go in and see, and there are

speaker: SS
words: places to picnic and it's really quite a delightful place. It's twelve

speaker: SS
words: miles south of Menomonie, Wisconsin, and it's called the Caddy Woodlawn

speaker: SS
words: Park, although, of course, the real name of the family was Woodhouse. It's

speaker: SS
words: interesting that really thousands of people go through there in the summer

speaker: SS
words: and sign their name in the guest book. That's been a great surprise and

speaker: SS
words: delight to me. And the people of Menomonie are very proud of this park.

speaker: SS
words: Every year they give the play of Caddy Woodlawn in an old pioneer theatre

speaker: SS
words: that they have in Menomonie, and that's partly how the funds are raised

speaker: SS
words: to keep the park in good repair, and it's also helped to finance other historical

speaker: SS
words: projects that they have. But I might have just written myself right

speaker: SS
words: out on a limb, not realizing that I was writing history at the time, but

speaker: SS
words: fortunately I didn't fictionalize it so much that people were unable to recognize

speaker: SS
words: local characters and places. So, while I have this historical back

speaker: SS
words: ground in Idaho, I also have this little spot in Wisconsin that's dedicated

speaker: SS
words: to the memory of my grandmother and the book, Caddy Woodlawn.

speaker: SS
words: Perhaps you would like to have me tell you something about my father

speaker: SS
words: and his brothers, who came from Scotland and settled in Moscow in the early

speaker: SS
words: days: Much of their story I have told in Snow In The River. But, there

speaker: SS
words: again, I have to caution people who are looking for real history to remember that

speaker: SS
words: where I wanted to make a good story I felt free to fictionalize, in

speaker: SS
words: this book as well as in others. But much of the story that is told in

speaker: SS
words: Snow In The River, about my father and his brothers is true. My father,

speaker: SS
words: Alexander Ryrie, was the third child of James and Charlotte Ryrie, and was

speaker: SS
words: born near Thurso in Scotland on August 24, 1865. His early training was in

speaker: SS
words: the church and common school and at thirteen he accepted a clerkship in

speaker: SS
words: the post office of Thurso. In July 1888 he arrived in Portland, Oregon

speaker: SS
words: with two friends from Scotland, and he stayed there for about a year. In

speaker: SS
words: 1889 he went to Moscow, Idaho, where he lived until his death, December

speaker: SS
words: 1, 1900. On November 8, 1893 he married Miss Henrietta Watkins, my mother.

speaker: SS
words: His two brothers joined him soon afterwards in Moscow and these three young

speaker: SS
words: Scotsmen were quite helpful in building up the town. They were young and

speaker: SS
words: lively and interested in the new country. My father went out as a representative for

speaker: SS
words: a Scotch life insurance company, I think it was the Balfour-

speaker: SS
words: Guthrie Company, and he also was in the real estate business and loans and

speaker: SS
words: mortgages and that sort of thing. And he did well in the new town. As I

speaker: SS
words: understand it, he became the first or one of the first mayors of the town.

speaker: SS
words: He'd learned surveying in Scotland and he superintended the laying out of

speaker: SS
words: many of the Moscow streets, and he rode around on horseback and named many

speaker: SS
words: of the streets. He was a very religious man, a devoted Presbyterian. Once

speaker: SS
words: he planned to enter the ministry but the family was too poor to finance the

speaker: SS
words: education of a Scottish minister. And he had thought of going as a missionary to Africa.

speaker: SS
words: That used to worry me when I was a child, I thought what if

speaker: SS
words: he had gone to Africa and would I be half black or would I be or would I

speaker: SS
words: have existed at all? It used to be quite a troublesome thought to me.

speaker: SS
words: In Moscow he always took a very active part in the church work and he was

speaker: SS
words: instrumental in getting a larger church building. For many years he made

speaker: SS
words: the weekly church calendars; he wrote them out in longhand and decorated

speaker: SS
words: them with little freehand drawings. He'd never had any training in art at

speaker: SS
words: all, but he had a good sense of drawing, and my mother used to help him with

speaker: SS
words: these and they mimeographed them then and handed them out to the congregation

speaker: SS
words: on Sunday. And they were prized so that for years people used to

speaker: SS
words: find them in their attics and old trunks in Moscow and give them to me, so

speaker: SS
words: I have quite a collection of them now. He was very active in Sunday School

speaker: SS
words: work and lots of the men, who were young men when I was a child, used to

speaker: SS
words: remember my father in that way. He died of tuberculosis— consumption in

speaker: SS
words: those days— which was an almost fatal disease. Old cookbooks that I have

speaker: SS
words: recommended the cure for it as powdered charcoal mixed up with honey and

speaker: SS
words: molasses or something of the sort— oh, port wine, I think. Anyway, they

speaker: SS
words: had no idea how to cure it but they were getting the idea that it was good

speaker: SS
words: to go and sit in the sun, and he did go to Hawaii, he and my mother went,

speaker: SS
words: in the days when the United States had just taken over the Hawaiian Islands. The old

speaker: SS
words: Queen Liliuokalani was still alive and they had many interesting recollections of

speaker: SS
words: that. That was the first time that I stayed with

speaker: SS
words: my grandparents and began a long association with my grandmother that was

speaker: SS
words: useful to me in later years.

speaker: SS
words: These early day things in Moscow— Moscow

speaker: SS
words: was quite a different town then; I have some old pictures of it, too, that

speaker: SS
words: show the old standpipe standing up against the mountains and the town

speaker: SS
words: itself looking very small and— well— unfinished, as it was in those days.

speaker: SS
words: Idaho, of course, was still a territory when my father and my grandfather

speaker: SS
words: came there. And there were many other Scottish people there. There used

speaker: SS
words: to be a boardinghouse run by an old Scottish widow and these young Scots

speaker: SS
words: men stayed there and she made a sort of home for them, and used to give

speaker: SS
words: Halloween parties and other entertainments that kept alive their social

speaker: SS
words: life. I think that people had to make their own social life in those days.

speaker: SS
words: They didn't have the movies or the television and all the things that we

speaker: SS
words: have to distract us now-a-days. And they really had a lot of fun. I can re

speaker: SS
words: member when I was researching a book on the Singing Hutchinsons, who were

speaker: SS
words: Civil War singers and had nothing to do with Idaho, but they founded a little town

speaker: SS
words: in Minnesota named Hutchinson, and I went down and interviewed

speaker: SS
words: some of the pioneers there, and one of the old women who talked to me, said,

speaker: SS
words: "Now, my dear, you're going to write about the pioneers, but don't tell all

speaker: SS
words: the hardships we had and none of the good times." She said, "Tell people

speaker: SS
words: when you write about them, that we had fun, too." And I've often thought

speaker: SS
words: of that, because I think we tend to think of the pioneers as having this

speaker: SS
words: terribly hard struggle in life, but not realizing that they had a lot of

speaker: SS
words: fun, too, at the same time.

speaker: SS
words: My mother was very much like her father, Dr. Watkins. She was very

speaker: SS
words: dark, very striking looking and quite a beauty. She was also a very good

speaker: SS
words: musician and used to play concerts around Moscow and belonged to a music

speaker: SS
words: al club and historical club and various clubs in town. And my aunt, also,

speaker: SS
words: was younger— she was very dear to me in my childhood and very important

speaker: SS
words: to me—she stayed at home and didn't marry until, well, when I was almost

speaker: SS
words: grown. But she had kind of an interesting character, too, and many people

speaker: SS
words: in Moscow still remember her as Elsie File, or Miss Elsie, as she was called, even

speaker: SS
words: after she was married.

speaker: SS
words: One of the things the young people used to do in Moscow in the summer

speaker: SS
words: was to go camping up in the mountains, up on Moscow Mountain. I have some

speaker: SS
words: interesting old pictures of young people who were out camping— they'd take

speaker: SS
words: a chaperone along, but it was all quite informal and very gay, and they

speaker: SS
words: don't look like young people on a camping trip now. The girls didn't wear

speaker: SS
words: bikinis or anything of that sort, they were very well covered, but they were

speaker: SS
words: all having a very good time, and I think that is one of the things to re

speaker: SS
words: member, that they had a lot of fun in those days as well as serious times.

speaker: SS
words: My uncle, Donald Ryrie and Henry Ryrie, also came from Scotland with my

speaker: SS
words: father and lived in Moscow in the early days. Another brother came later,

speaker: SS
words: but he never got used to the fact that the men had to milk the cows on the

speaker: SS
words: farms and that the women were more independent than they were in Scotland

speaker: SS
words: and he went back and built a little farmhouse near my grandfather's place

speaker: SS
words: in Northern Scotland and didn't even put a modern cook stove in it, they

speaker: SS
words: cooked over an open fireplace, so he was a real throwback to the old peasant days.

speaker: SS
words: But my other two uncles became complete Americans. My Uncle

speaker: SS
words: Don was my great hero as a child. I had great love for him and he is the

speaker: SS
words: one I have called Douglas in Snow In The River.

speaker: SS
words: My parents' house was right across the street from the Frank David

speaker: SS
words: house and my mother and Mrs. David were great friends. And Donald David,

speaker: SS
words: who has since achieved quite a career in business in the East, he and I

speaker: SS
words: were born about the same time and our mothers used to have us photographed

speaker: SS
words: together very often. I have photographs of us. when we pore probably about

speaker: SS
words: four years old with big McKinley and Roosevelt campaign buttons, that is,

speaker: SS
words: Teddy Roosevelt. I don't think that we were old enough to have many political views

speaker: SS
words: of our own, but I am sure this must have reflected the views

speaker: SS
words: of our parents. But in that connection, it's rather interesting— a little

speaker: SS
words: vignette of memory that I have— I was about five years old, I suppose,

speaker: SS
words: and I can remember sitting at a dressing table in my grandmother's bedroom

speaker: SS
words: and somebody was brushing my hair, my mother or my aunt, I don't know who,

speaker: SS
words: and I remember that somebody rushed in and said, "The President has been

speaker: SS
words: shot! President McKinley has been an anarchist, I believe. Well, the consternation

speaker: SS
words: of the adults communicated itself to me and I remember this very

speaker: SS
words: clearly, although it's a tiny vignette that is completely surrounded by

speaker: SS
words: fog and haze. I just see it like a little picture. But that's my one connection with

speaker: SS
words: the history of the period.

speaker: SS
words: As I was growing up, we had no Girl Scouts, but the Sunday School class

speaker: SS
words: used to take the place of Girl Scouts. We had a group of about twelve girls

speaker: SS
words: who attended Sunday School together and for a long time we had a very dear

speaker: SS
words: teacher, Mrs. Mattie Heddington, should be remembered in the town— she

speaker: SS
words: was a very wonderful woman. I think she had lost a husband and children

speaker: SS
words: in some rather tragic way, and she devoted herself to the children of town.

speaker: SS
words: She was a teacher and also a very good Sunday School teacher, and we used to

speaker: SS
words: go out on all sorts of exclusions and do most of the things that Girl Scouts

speaker: SS
words: do now-a-days. I can remember once that we camped out in the country in a

speaker: SS
words: farmer's barn— I can still remember how scratchy the straw was, trying to

speaker: SS
words: sleep in it! An old-time fiddler played for us to dance in an old abandoned

speaker: SS
words: house, I can't remember how we gathered the fiddler and the house and every

speaker: SS
words: thing together, but it was great fun. And, of course, we used to walk out

speaker: SS
words: the railroad tracks in the spring when the roads were all too deep in mud

speaker: SS
words: for any proper walking and get the earliest pussy willows and bring them

speaker: SS
words: back in triumph. And we did all sorts of things that weren't quite Sunday

speaker: SS
words: Schoolish. I can remember that we used to to try to raise people on our

speaker: SS
words: fingers— have somebody lie across a couple of chairs and we'd all breathe

speaker: SS
words: together and try to raise them up holding our fingers under them! Well,

speaker: SS
words: that doesn't sound very Sunday Schoolish, but it was great fun, and we did

speaker: SS
words: all sorts of things. We also had costume parties, I tell about one of those

speaker: SS
words: in Two Are Better Than One, when Charlotte and I dressed as rag dolls and

speaker: SS
words: all the other girls went in beautiful costumes with their hair curled and

speaker: SS
words: their faces colored, and after the masquerade was over they took off their

speaker: SS
words: little masks and pulled off our big heavy rag doll heads stuffed with

speaker: SS
words: cotton and we were all sweaty and our hair was all sticking out on end

speaker: SS
words: with bunches of cotton stuck in it, and none of the boys would ask us to

speaker: SS
words: dance. We were sort of horrified by that, but we learned then that boys

speaker: SS
words: like pretty girls and not girls who dress up like rag dolls!!

speaker: SS
words: You asked about the homesteading near Clarkia and the Clearwater country.

speaker: SS
words: It's true, I believe, that the lumber companies were very interested in

speaker: SS
words: settling homesteaders in there with the idea that they would sell out and

speaker: SS
words: the lumber companies would be able to go in and cut out and get out— as

speaker: SS
words: they used to say . I think that the Conservationists did a great job by

speaker: SS
words: making this forest reserve, because otherwise, Idaho would have been pretty well devastated

speaker: SS
words: with it's big timber cut away. However, it was very hard

speaker: SS
words: on the homesteaders who went in expecting to make money on it, and losing

speaker: SS
words: out their claims in the end, because they couldn't prove that they were do

speaker: SS
words: ing it to make a home. That was the criteria, I believe that they held up

speaker: SS
words: for them to aim for. But it was a very interesting experience for my aunt

speaker: SS
words: and for some of us girls who went in with her sometimes in the summer.

speaker: SS
words: She had a homestead, I think it was about twenty-eight miles from Clarkia,

speaker: SS
words: which was the last post office and last wagon road. We packed everything

speaker: SS
words: in on pack horses. Aunt went in many times and it is remarkable as you mentioned,

speaker: SS
words: that so many of the homesteaders were women who went in on this

speaker: SS
words: venture. They expected to make some money out of it, and it wasn't so easy

speaker: SS
words: for women to make money in those days, and this was a chance they took. My

speaker: SS
words: aunt always said that she was a very kind of delicate, fearful, sickly young

speaker: SS
words: woman when she went in there, and after, I don't know whether it was four

speaker: SS
words: or five years of homesteading, she came out feeling much healthier, much

speaker: SS
words: more confident, much happier, and in fact, had, according to her own estimate, it

speaker: SS
words: had changed her whole character and outlook on life. Two summers

speaker: SS
words: she took me into the homestead with her. She didn't attempt to stay there

speaker: SS
words: in the winter, but they used to have to go in in order to prove, or they hoped

speaker: SS
words: it would prove, that they were making a home there. They would go in some

speaker: SS
words: times in the winter, and one winter she went in and it was snowed in and froze

speaker: SS
words: her feet on the way, they lost their way. When she got to the cabin she

speaker: SS
words: couldn't walk back out, her feet were too badly swollen, so they left her

speaker: SS
words: her there while they went back to get more help— I don't just understand

speaker: SS
words: what they were going to do— get horses, or something, and she had to stay

speaker: SS
words: there alone for a while and she remembered hearing the cougars yell— they're

speaker: SS
words: supposed to cry like a woman's scream— and it's quite blood-curdling, I

speaker: SS
words: guess. And she saw their tracks coming around to her window where they had

speaker: SS
words: sniffed at the window. Well, it was a very rugged experience for her. And

speaker: SS
words: some of the things that really happened to her I e put into Strangers in the

speaker: SS
words: Forest. Although, actually, that is a made-up plot. But the young girl who

speaker: SS
words: goes in and has some of these harrowing experiences, is really based on my

speaker: SS
words: aunt and her experiences. One summer she took four of us girls, we were

speaker: SS
words: in high school then, she took Charlotte Lewis, and Katherine France, and

speaker: SS
words: Lucille Watkins. Lucille was a great friend of mine, she is no relation in

speaker: SS
words: spite of the name Watkins. Her father was a dentist in town. And the four

speaker: SS
words: of us were great friends at school, and we went in on packhorses and did

speaker: SS
words: the whole bit, and it was really a great experience for all of us. The next

speaker: SS
words: summer I went in with her alone, and it seemed to me the most lonely summer

speaker: SS
words: I'd ever spent, and I wasn't very happy about it. I was glad to come back

speaker: SS
words: again. But it was most beautiful country. I can't begin to say how beautiful. I've

speaker: SS
words: not seen it since, and I don't know whether it's still kept its

speaker: SS
words: beauty. They've had many forest fires. In fact, one of them, while she

speaker: SS
words: was still there, and she escaped from it. But, I think that it is a very

speaker: SS
words: marvelous country and Idaho should be proud of it. And, also, I'm very glad

speaker: SS
words: that it is being preserved as a forest reserve. I tried to make the book

speaker: SS
words: as accurate as I could, and when it was condensed in the Reader's Digest

speaker: SS
words: Condensed Book they had got a note from the Chief Forester of the United

speaker: SS
words: States at that time, who wrote a little recommendation for the book, and

speaker: SS
words: apparently he thought well of it, so this probably is as authentic as it

speaker: SS
words: could be made.

speaker: SS
words: We used to stop at Bovill on our way to Clarkia, and then we had a stage— I think

speaker: SS
words: the train went as far as Bovill, I'm not sure, but then we

speaker: SS
words: took a stage from there to Clarkia. And I can remember the Bovill family.

speaker: SS
words: They were very interesting English people who had quite a— for that place

speaker: SS
words: and time—rather sophisticated hotel there at this little town of Bovill,

speaker: SS
words: and there were two daughters, Gwen— I can't remember the other one's name

speaker: SS
words: now— later they came up to the University when I was there, and I knew

speaker: SS
words: them then. But this was a very interesting family, I think he had come from

speaker: SS
words: one of the noble families in England, and he and his wife apparently loved

speaker: SS
words: the rough frontier life that they found out in Bovill.

speaker: SS
words: The summer that my aunt took the four of us into the woods with her,

speaker: SS
words: we were quite a curiosity to the people of Clarkia, to the lumberjacks and

speaker: SS
words: the man at the store had a big peace pipe made out of wood— I don't know

speaker: SS
words: whether it had really belonged to the Indians— but anyway, admired this

speaker: SS
words: greatly and he said he would give it to the girl who gained the most that

speaker: SS
words: summer. So we were all vying for the honor of earning the Indian pipe.

speaker: SS
words: Now-a-days ladies all want to vie to see who can remain the thinnest, but

speaker: SS
words: we were at a very young age and we were interested in seeing who could gain

speaker: SS
words: the most. We had a lot of wonderful food out there. Fresh trout, that even

speaker: SS
words: we could catch with a little fishline tied to a pole and a hook on the end

speaker: SS
words: of it, the trout were so plentiful at that time. And then there were al

speaker: SS
words: ways the sourdough bucket with wonderful sourdough pancakes. And Charlotte

speaker: SS
words: Lewis was the tallest and thinnest of the four of us, and she had the great

speaker: SS
words: est capacity for pancakes and that summer she gained the most, so when we

speaker: SS
words: came back to town and we were all weighed, she won the Indian pipe. There

speaker: SS
words: were a lot of interesting characters in there: we had a lumberjack working

speaker: SS
words: for us who helped to make the garden, it was essential that you had to have

speaker: SS
words: a little garden, even if the deer ate it up or the other critters destroyed

speaker: SS
words: it, but that was part of the fiction that you were going to make a home out

speaker: SS
words: on this homestead. And this lumberjack who worked for us was named Dynamite. I don't

speaker: SS
words: remember his real name but everybody called him Dynamite,

speaker: SS
words: and this was because he told a wonderful story about being in Seattle once

speaker: SS
words: when a building was burning, and they had, I don't know how many pounds of

speaker: SS
words: dynamite in a great package on one of the upper floors, and somebody called

speaker: SS
words: out to him to catch it and they threw it out of the upper window and he caught

speaker: SS
words: the package of dynamite and it didn't explode. Well, I am sure that that was

speaker: SS
words: one of his pleasant fictions, but anyway, he got the name of Dynamite by that.

speaker: SS
words: This was really a taste of the pioneer life for us who had lived in town al

speaker: SS
words: ways. We learned to ford streams on horseback and to climb mountains, and it

speaker: SS
words: was a great life. There were lots of wild huckleberries and the bears would

speaker: SS
words: come around and we'd hear them in the huckleberry patches, but we never did

speaker: SS
words: really have an encounter with one. We used to meet snakes on the trail, and

speaker: SS
words: that didn't please us because we were all kind of squeamish about snakes. We

speaker: SS
words: were real little town girls. But it was an interesting adventure and much

speaker: SS
words: of that has gone in as background to Strangers in the Forest, although the

speaker: SS
words: story itself is fiction.

speaker: SS
words: You asked me whether I felt that I had been helped or hindered by society.

speaker: SS
words: I think I have always been helped. I have been very fortunate and had a very

speaker: SS
words: happy life in general. I have— well, I think that writing is a wonderful

speaker: SS
words: occupation for a woman if she likes to do it. It's something you can do and

speaker: SS
words: keep a home and have a family and you don't have to give up any of the pleasures of

speaker: SS
words: being a woman, and still you can write. Now I must say that I owe

speaker: SS
words: a great deal of my freedom to write and do as I like to my husband, who was

speaker: SS
words: always my best supporter and very loyal in whatever I did, and helped me

speaker: SS
words: in every way. He was a writer too. He was a mathematician, and his books

speaker: SS
words: were mysteries to me, I could never read them. He could read mine and tell

speaker: SS
words: me what was wrong with them. So we used to have a kind of joke about that.

speaker: SS
words: But we have the same working habits. He was a professor of mathematics for

speaker: SS
words: many years at the University of Minnesota. He had come out to Idaho on his

speaker: SS
words: first job when he was just nineteen and he was trying to look very dignified

speaker: SS
words: because he was younger than some of his students. He taught in the prep

speaker: SS
words: school at the University. We met— at that time I was six years younger

speaker: SS
words: and still mostly a young hobbledehoy, but somehow we kept in touch for the

speaker: SS
words: years when he was away getting a graduate degree and I was in college, and

speaker: SS
words: it was a most fortunate— a happy combination all of our lives. Our ideas

speaker: SS
words: and our feeling toward life were so similar. I feel sorry for husbands and

speaker: SS
words: wives who don't have this particular friendship along with the love and other

speaker: SS
words: instincts. It's a kind of wonderful thing to be a good friend to your hus

speaker: SS
words: band or wife, too. And we did have such a relationship. So I owe much of

speaker: SS
words: my freedom to write and my pleasure in it to his indulgence and his inter

speaker: SS
words: est. I have never felt handicapped as a woman. Perhaps if I had tried to

speaker: SS
words: go out and work in some competitive job with a man— a writer is in a way

speaker: SS
words: a competitor with other writers, but it's something that you do alone

speaker: SS
words: on your own and you can express yourself freely, and I've never felt any

speaker: SS
words: handicap in being a woman. I have enjoyed being a woman and have not wished

speaker: SS
words: to change my status at all.

speaker: SS
words: I don't know that I have answered all of your questions, and perhaps I

speaker: SS
words: had better leave this tape and play it over again and see what I have left

speaker: SS
words: out and what I can add to it at a later date. But for now I'm going to sign

speaker: SS
words: off.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BRINK READS SKETCHES, WHICH HAVE BEEN DELETED FROM THIS TRANSCRIPTION

speaker: SS
words: I have still not finished the tape, and so in conclusion I'll say one

speaker: SS
words: or two more things that are on my mind. It seems to me that to have lived eighty years,

speaker: SS
words: as I have almost done,

speaker: SS
words: is a privilege increasingly common in our time. Soon, perhaps, it'll be

speaker: SS
words: nothing to live until ninety or a hundred. This is fine if there is no

speaker: SS
words: diminution of power, no loss of wonder. If we live only to nurse our aches

speaker: SS
words: and pains— the doctors have been to a lot of trouble for no good purpose—

speaker: SS
words: we are better dead. The only hope for the aged is that they may have learned

speaker: SS
words: wisdom. If they can distil some good wine out of the crushed grape$then

speaker: SS
words: all the miracles of modern medicine may be worth while. What appears to us

speaker: SS
words: bit by bit when we are young may be seen as a whole when we are old. We see

speaker: SS
words: how the pieces of the jigsaw puzzle fit in finally until the whole is complete. If

speaker: SS
words: there are pieces that do not dovetail, we at least have more perspective in which

speaker: SS
words: to view them. So to look backward need not be entirely

speaker: SS
words: futile. I am glad to remember a peaceful and innocent time when many people

speaker: SS
words: born later than I have been will never know. It's pleasant for me to look

speaker: SS
words: back on it, to see a life through and marvel at it. This, I suppose, is one

speaker: SS
words: one of the chief solaces of age. Also, I think, that it is a privilege to

speaker: SS
words: grow up in a small town. The large world outside is usually concentrated in

speaker: SS
words: a small town and is more easily observed. All of the virtues and vices of

speaker: SS
words: the large world blossom in a small town with the most superb intensity. In

speaker: SS
words: this drop of water under a microscope you can observe all nature and become

speaker: SS
words: acquainted with people of all walks of life. Cities are too one-sided and diffuse.

speaker: SS
words: The section in which you live in a city may represent only one phase of life or activity.

speaker: SS
words: But the child who has grown up with open eyes in a little town has inherited the earth.

speaker: SS
words: I should like to end with greetings and love to the old friends who

speaker: SS
words: still live in Moscow, and to wish you all many happy days.
